description: S&P Global is a financial information, ratings, benchmarks, analytics, and workflow-data company serving capital markets, commodity and energy markets, and automotive intelligence customers.

overview.title: Business Snapshot
paragraphs: S&P Global sits near the center of modern market infrastructure: it rates debt, publishes benchmark indices, sells market and company data, and provides analytics that help institutions decide what risk is worth taking. Its core divisions include S&P Global Ratings, S&P Dow Jones Indices, Market Intelligence, Energy, and Mobility.

The company’s influence is unusually durable because many of its products become embedded in regulation, investment mandates, bond issuance workflows, and index-linked capital allocation. When finance says it wants neutral infrastructure, it often means a subscription terminal, a licensed benchmark, and a bill from someone like S&P Global.

overview.title: Product Surface
paragraphs: S&P Global Ratings provides credit ratings, research, and risk opinions for issuers and debt markets. S&P Dow Jones Indices maintains widely used benchmarks, including equity and fixed-income index families that support ETFs, mutual funds, structured products, and institutional performance measurement.

Beyond the two seed products, the registry should likely track S&P Global Market Intelligence for financial data and desktop/workflow tools, S&P Global Energy for commodity and energy pricing intelligence, and S&P Global Mobility for automotive market data and forecasting. These businesses make S&P Global less a single ratings agency than a portfolio of decision infrastructure for capital-heavy industries.

overview.title: Open Alternative Angle
paragraphs: The strongest Free The World framing is not that every S&P Global product has a simple free replacement. Credit ratings, investable benchmarks, and institutional-grade pricing data carry network effects, compliance dependencies, and licensing moats that are difficult to replicate with a volunteer spreadsheet and optimism.

The more realistic alternative map should separate use cases: public company fundamentals, open market data, transparent index methodologies, community-maintained datasets, decentralized oracle feeds, open credit-risk models, and public-sector disclosure repositories. That split will make the registry useful instead of pretending that one open project can replace a regulated benchmark empire overnight.
